comment_content: Not sure why people seem to ignore the fact that the Expose were never good. They had 1 playoff appearance in 35 years (give or take). That’s not a good team/franchise.
reply_content:
Completely untrue.  Yes, they were bad their first ten years in the league from 1969-1978.  In 1979 they had their first winning season, going 95-65, and finishing in 2nd place to the eventual champion Pittsburgh Pirates.  In 1980 they came close again but finished in 2nd to the eventual champion Philadelphia Phillies.  In 1981 they made their only playoff appearance and lost in heartbreaking fashion on Blue Monday to the eventual champion L.A. Dodgers.  This is why Montrealers began viewing the Expos as snake-bit bridesmaids.  They continued to field talented teams for years but could never get over the hump.

But not a good team? Completely false.  From 1979-1994 no team won more regular season games than the Montreal Expos. You don't lead MLB in wins over a 16-year span without being pretty good.  Of course, we know what happened in 1994, which was the crushing blow that set the wheels in motion for their ultimate demise. After that, they managed only 3 winning seasons in their final 10 years of existence.

But consider these names and the talent level of the players on this list:  Rusty Staub, Ken Singleton, Steve Rogers, Gary Carter, Larry Parrish, Andre Dawson, Ellis Valentine, Warren Cromartie, Bill Gullickson, Jeff Reardon, Al Oliver, Tim Wallach, Andres Galarraga, Dennis Martinez, Randy Johnson, Ken Hill, Delino DeShields, Larry Walker, Marquis Grissom, Moises Alou, Cliff Floyd, Rondell White, Pedro Martinez, David Segui, Henry Rodriguez, Vladimir Guerrero, Javier Vazquez, Jose Vidro, Orlando Cabrera, etc.  That's just a partial list of great players that were developed by the Expos or became stars in Montreal.  I could name dozens more. 

Bottom line:  Anyone who says the Expos were never good has no clue what they're talking about.  They were just never champions, and the fact that they had their best chance ever ripped away in 1994 makes that fact all the more painful to Expos fans.

comment_content: Im new to basbeall so im not sure but would people compare the '94 expos to the '85 chicago bears?
reply_content:
It's an interesting question.  The 1985 Chicago Bears went 18-1 and won the Super Bowl in dominating fashion.  Not too many teams in any sport were ever that dominant in one season, but the 1994 Expos were not only the best team in franchise history but easily one of the best teams I've ever seen in my 45+ years of watching baseball, and were getting stronger as the season went on. They finished with a 74-40 record after starting 4-9. They won 20 of their last 23 games and were on pace for 105 wins. 

Their outfield was amazing and one of the best of all time -- Larry Walker, Marquis Grissom and Moises Alou.  As good a trio offensively, defensively and on the basepaths that you could ask for.  They say speed never slumps, and the whole team could run.

The ace of the staff was Ken Hill, but Jeff Fassero and Butch Henry were also outstanding, and so was a young fireballer named Pedro Martinez.  The bullpen was full of great arms like John Wetteland, Mel Rojas and Tim Scott.  They had 5 all-stars that year, including catcher Darrin Fletcher and shortstop Wil Cordero.  Grissom homered in that all-star game and Alou had the game-winning double.  Future all-stars like Rondell White and Cliff Floyd were also on that team, and so were a lot of other great contributors and/or future stars like Mike Lansing, Sean Berry, Kirk Rueter, Gil Heredia and Jeff Shaw. 

On top of all that great talent, the team had great team chemistry and a great manager in Felipe Alou.  And when the Big O was packed, there was no louder stadium or better homefield advantage. They were packing that stadium consistently when the strike hit, and lost out on 29 home games they still had remaining on the schedule.  When you consider that 29 of the remaining 48 games on the schedule were to be played at home, I feel like 105 wins was the minimum they would've achieved.  They were that good, and inspired that much confidence. 

When you look at the talent on that 1994 Expos team and the careers most of those guys went on to have, there was clearly the potential for a dynasty if that team had been kept together.  So to answer your question, the 1985 Bears may have been more dominant in that one season, but they didn't sustain that level of greatness in future seasons.  I'm convinced that Expos team, if kept together, would've won multiple titles. Pedro Martinez has said that if the team hadn't been broken up by fire sales, the Yankees wouldn't be talking about how many titles they won in the 1990's because those championships would be in Montreal. @natster_ It would've been great if the Expos could've signed Pedro long-term when he first emerged as a star in 1994, but by then ownership was already counting every penny because they knew there was a lot of young talent on the team that was going to become unaffordable  After the strike cancelled the end of the 1994 season, Expos ownership got rid of four of the team's greatest stars in a cost-cutting move: Marquis Grissom, Larry Walker, Ken Hill and John Wetteland, otherwise known as their gold-glove centerfielder and speedster leadoff hitter (Grissom), their gold-glove Canadian-born future Hall of Fame 5-tool rightfielder and cleanup hitter (Walker), their staff ace and best starting pitcher at the time (Hill) and their star closer and future World Series MVP with the Yankees (Wetteland).  

To your point, Walker has stated in interviews that he talked with some of the other guys and they agreed to stay together for less money to allow themselves another chance in 1995 if given the opportunity, but ownership didn't even offer Walker a contract and traded the other three.  

 Pedro's final year as an Expo was actually 1997 when he emerged as a truly legendary pitcher, striking out over 300 batters and having a miniscule ERA on his way to his first of many Cy Young Awards, but the team had a disappointing second half and finished below .500. After 1997, the Expos had their second most damaging "fire sale", getting rid of Martinez, Darrin Fletcher, Mike Lansing, David Segui and Henry Rodriguez.  From that point on it seemed like their days in Montreal were numbered.  The final killshot happened when Vlad Guerrero Sr. left for the Angels after exciting seasons in 2002 and 2003, when the Expos defied all odds to stay in contention for long stretches of each season, before eventually finishing with only 83 wins each season.  Vlad was the team's final true superstar, and when he left all hope seemed to go with him.  But the wheels were put in motion for the Expos death ten years prior with the way that 1994 season ended.

I hope you get a chance one day to see a baseball team come back to Montreal.  I would be very motivated to move back to Montreal if that day ever came.  As you mentioned, Olympic Stadium was a big part of the problem, so they would need a new stadium and a good, deep-pocketed ownership group who would be willing to invest in the team, unlike the cheapskates who let Expos fans down the first time.  It's a longshot, but I haven't given up hope.  If Rogers can own the Blue Jays, Bell should step up and invest in the return of the Expos.

comment_content:
Can't wait to see this.  As a kid in Connecticut, I was a Red Sox fan, but being a little OCD I needed a favorite team to root for in each division - in the NL East it was the Expos.  No idea why - I guess they seemed exotic in some way, and I had an old hand-me-down souvenir sweatshirt from Expo '67.  All I know is I loved Carter, Dawson, Valentine, Speier, Parrish, Rogers and the rest.  Blue Monday killed me only slightly less than Bucky Dent's HR in '78.  

I've been in DC since 1986, and while I was thrilled to get a club, I was heart sick is was the Expos.  There are many villains in the story of the Expos departure from Montreal, but make no mistake - this was no accident; it was a deliberate hit.  In my less lucid periods, I wonder whether the cancellation of the 1994 post season wasn't part of MLB's effort to stymie any chance the Expos had of being champions and to gain a wider following.  

One of the other commenters has it right - they need to do another one on the A's.

comment_content: There are two aspects of the 94 strike that always make me mad & go what if. If there is no strike does Tony Gwynn hit .400 & would the Montreal Expos still be around? Yes I know that Matt Williams was flirting with Maris' record but that has been broken. Hitting .400 is the holy grail that will likely never happen & The Expos were a damn good team with tons of talent. If they had the right owner who knows?
reply_content:
Agreed. Apart from what happened to the Expos, Tony Gwynn's lost opportunity to hit .400 was one of the most painful consequences of the strike. In reality, losing everything after August 12th sucked.  It completely tarnished an otherwise great season of baseball, and left us with countless what-ifs. 

As an Expos fan, just being deprived of seeing such an awesome team play the final six weeks of the season alone was a tragedy.  Even if they'd resumed the season in time for the playoffs and the Expos had won it all, a big part of me still would've felt extremely short-changed by not being able to see that great Expos team play the full season.  But the way it played out, with the cancellation of the season and the dismantling of the Expos roster before the 1995 season began was beyond brutal, and the ultimate slap in the face.
